text: The surgeon initiates dissection below the arcuate line, avoiding the posterior rectus fascia during the transabdominal preperitoneal hernia repair.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum sharply using electrocautery, creating a visible opening in the tissue.

The surgeon applies traction in the posterior and medial direction, allowing insufflation to enter the dissection plane, visibly facilitating its development and maintaining the correct anatomical plane.
The surgeon aims to stay within the layers of the transverse allus fascia, which is visible as the properitoneal layer during the dissection.
The surgeon dissects in the inferior direction for a short distance before extending the flap laterally, avoiding a tunnel-like operative field.

The surgeon carefully manages the depth of the dissection flap to avoid creating a hole in the peritoneal flap or invading the retro-rectus plane, which would expose the rectus abdominal muscle along with its associated vessels and small nerves.
The surgeon approaches the lateral dissection, carefully following the dissection plane as it turns posteriorly, avoiding the muscle plane.
The surgeon uses scissors pointed downward to perform blunt dissection laterally, preserving the anatomical plane and maintaining the fat layer overlying the nerves in the area.
The surgeon identifies the dissection plane above a small splotch of blood, which leads towards the nerves and the triangle of pain.
The surgeon maintains the correct dissection plane close to the flap, ensuring a layer of fat remains between the surgical mesh and the underlying nerves.
Following the completion of the lateral dissection, the surgeon proceeds to dissect medially towards the deep inguinal ring, maintaining the correct plane without the use of electrocautery.
The video shows a preserved layer of tissue overlying the abdominal wall muscle and the inferior epigastric vessels, highlighting the anatomical structures in this area.
The video fast-forwards past the dissection of the hernia sac and medial dissection, resuming at the final portion of the lateral dissection.
The surgeon frees the gonadal vessels from the lateral peritoneal flap, creating space for the mesh placement. This is achieved through blunt or sharp dissection, avoiding the use of electrocautery.
The surgeon deploys the mesh at the end of the operation, adjusting its placement within the pocket created during the dissection.
The surgeon places a ten by fifteen centimeter pro grip mesh into the prepared pocket, ensuring it fits smoothly without any folds or kinks.
